steps.prompt: Which word is the first verb?

hints.Denique: 'Denique' is an adverb meaning 'finally / in short'
hints.animo: 'animo' is a noun in the ablative meaning 'with this spirit/attitude'
hints.desperetis: 'desperetis' is a verb but in the subordinate 'ut' clause

hints.Present imperative: Present imperative of 'esse' is 'este' — 'estote' with -tote ending is the future imperative
hints.Present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'sitis' — 'estote' is a rare future imperative form
hints.Future indicative: Future indicative would be 'eritis' — 'estote' is the future imperative

hints.sto, stare, steti, statum: These are the parts of 'stare' (to stand)
hints.eo, ire, ii, itum: These are the parts of 'ire' (to go)
hints.edo, edere, edi, esum: These are the parts of 'edere' (to eat)

hints.Present indicative: Present indicative would be 'desperatis' — 'desperetis' is subjunctive
hints.Imperfect subjunctive: Imperfect subjunctive would be 'desperaretis' — 'desperetis' is present
hints.Perfect subjunctive: Perfect subjunctive would be 'desperaveritis' — 'desperetis' is present

steps.target: Result clause after 'isto animo … ut' — 'of such a spirit that …'
choices: Result clause after 'isto animo … ut' — 'of such a spirit that …'

hints.Purpose clause: Purpose clauses express intention — 'isto animo … ut' describes the character of their spirit (result)
hints.Indirect command: Indirect commands follow verbs of ordering — this follows 'estote' with a description of attitude
hints.Indirect question: Indirect questions use interrogative words — 'ut' here introduces a result clause

hints.spero, sperare, speravi, speratum: These are the parts of 'sperare' (to hope) — the base verb without de- prefix
hints.aspero, asperare, asperavi, asperatum: These would be the parts of 'asperare' (to roughen) — a different verb
hints.prospero, prosperare, prosperavi, prospe: These would be the parts of 'prosperare' (to prosper) — a different verb

hints.Nominative: Nominative would be 'animus' — 'animo' is ablative of quality/description
hints.Dative: Dative would be 'animo' (same form), but with 'isto' and the context it is ablative of quality

hints.1st: 1st declension ablative ends in -a — 'animus, -i' is 2nd declension
hints.3rd: 3rd declension ablative ends in -e — 'animus, -i' is 2nd declension
hints.4th: 4th declension ablative ends in -u — 'animus, -i' is 2nd declension

translation: Finally, be of such a spirit that you give up all hope.
